ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Gong Bao deer meat. FOODON_03302051 (stir-fried food) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers deer meat as a raw ingredient category. No specific code exists for the Gong Bao stir-fried preparation; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 identifies venison as a substance. No procedure-specific code for Gong Bao stir-fry preparation exists; closest_parent applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Stir-Fried Gong Bao Deer Meat is approximately 250 g, comprising seasoned venison pieces with peanuts, dried chillies, and aromatics, served as a shared main dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17175); adapted for Gong Bao stir-fried preparation with added oil, soy sauce, peanuts, and sugar
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw venison values (USDA FoodData Central, NDB 17175 / FDC ID 174491) were used as the protein and fat base. Adjustments were made for stir-frying with vegetable oil (~8 g fat added per 100 g cooked dish), soy sauce (~400 mg sodium added per 100 g), peanuts (~3 g fat, ~2 g protein, ~1 g carbohydrate per 100 g dish), sugar and cornstarch (~4 g carbohydrate per 100 g dish). Energy was recalculated using Atwater factors. Values represent the cooked dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat stir-fry with low-GI sauce components
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gong Bao deer meat. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed meat stir-fry dishes with predominantly protein and fat content and low carbohydrate load. Venison is a lean protein with negligible glycemic contribution; the small carbohydrate fraction (cornstarch, sugar, peanuts) is diluted across the dish. Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN) provides the reference framework for analogous protein-dominant stir-fry GI estimation. GL per serving = (35 × 5.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce (~520 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 35) and low carbohydrate content (5.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-Fried Gong Bao Deer Meat has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with portion-controlled rice and monitored for added sugar in the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (520 mg per 100 g); partially offset by lean venison protein and favourable unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Venison is a lean red meat with a relatively favourable fat profile; however, the sodium content from soy sauce and the use of cooking oil elevate cardiovascular risk, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request low-sodium soy sauce alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Gong Bao Deer Meat (宫保鹿肉, Gōng Bǎo Lù Ròu) is a Chinese stir-fried dish that adapts the classic Sichuan Gong Bao (Kung Pao) cooking technique to venison (deer meat) as the primary protein. The dish is prepared by marinating diced venison in soy sauce, rice wine, and cornstarch, then wok-frying at high heat with dried red chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, and roasted peanuts. The sauce is a balanced blend of soy sauce, rice wine, sugar, and vinegar, producing the characteristic sweet-sour-spicy-numbing flavour profile associated with Gong Bao cuisine. Venison is a lean, dark-red game meat with a firm texture and mild gamey flavour that absorbs the bold Sichuan seasonings well. The dish is served as a shared main course alongside steamed jasmine rice in Chinese restaurant settings in Singapore. Nutritionally, venison is notably high in protein (~18.5 g per 100 g cooked dish), relatively low in fat compared to beef or pork, and rich in iron (2.8 mg), zinc (3.2 mg), and vitamin B12 (2.1 mcg), making it a nutrient-dense red meat option. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from soy sauce (~520 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is low (estimated GI 35) due to the protein-dominant macronutrient profile and minimal carbohydrate content. This dish is suitable for individuals managing blood glucose but requires sodium monitoring for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with peanut or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Gong Bao deer meat exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values were adapted from USDA FoodData Central raw venison data (FDC ID 174491) with calculated adjustments for stir-frying with oil, soy sauce, peanuts, and sugar. GI is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant stir-fry dishes per Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central venison entry and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; venison naturally contains negligible industrial trans fats, and no hydrogenated oils are used in this preparation — confirmed zero by formulation. 3. Nutrient values represent the cooked dish per 100 g after stir-frying adjustments. 4. Halal status is marked false as deer meat halal certification depends on the slaughter method, which is not confirmed for this restaurant source. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON (Seafood Dish, Crab, Lobster) appear to be erroneous metadata from the source platform and have been disregarded; this dish contains no seafood. 6. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant stir-fry framework; no direct GI measurement for Gong Bao venison was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. GL per serving = (35 × 5.5 g carbohydrate × 250 g serving) / 10000 = 4.8. 8. Hindi local name uses Devanagari script with a Chinese character suffix (炒 = stir-fry) as a common transliteration convention; the exact string गोंग बाओ हिरण मांस炒 appears in both aliases_by_language and the flat aliases list.
